does: This paper develops a systematic benchmark to evaluate environmental attitudes embedded in large language models across three dimensions: cognition, affect, and behavioural recommendations. The authors test 31 proprietary and open-weight LLMs using questions adapted from established environmental awareness surveys, comparing model responses both among themselves and against human survey data from Germany. They assess response robustness across different prompting conditions. The findings reveal that many LLMs express environmentally progressive attitudes exceeding those of average survey respondents, demonstrating higher environmental cognition and affect while recommending behaviours with substantial CO2 reduction potential. However, the analysis uncovers no systematic correlation between these sustainability-oriented responses and observable model characteristics such as origin, size, or training approach.
connects: This work builds on emerging concerns about value alignment in large language models, extending beyond safety and fairness to environmental cognition specifically. It draws on established environmental psychology and survey methodology—particularly environmental awareness instruments used in sustainability research—to operationalize what environmental attitudes mean computationally. The paper responds to growing deployment of LLMs in climate and sustainability contexts where their embedded values could influence organizational and individual decision-making, building on prior work examining whether LLMs reliably represent particular value systems. It connects to broader investigations of how training data, scale, and instruction-tuning shape model outputs on normative questions.
enables: This benchmark framework enables future systematic auditing of LLM environmental attitudes as these models evolve and become more integrated into sustainability reporting, carbon accounting, and climate communication tools. The work opens questions about whether apparent environmental progressiveness is robust or merely reflects surface-level patterns in training data. It points toward investigating mechanisms driving these attitudes—whether they emerge from specific datasets, instruction-tuning practices, or RLHF approaches. The null findings regarding model characteristics suggest opportunity for deeper mechanistic analysis and controlled studies isolating which training decisions produce environmental values. Ultimately this foundation could inform governance of LLM deployment in high-stakes sustainability contexts and guide development of models with reliably calibrated environmental reasoning.
